Sentence: You step off the bus and the world exhales salt.
masts become ghosts,
tarred ropes melt into gray.
You pull the collar of your olive jacket up and tuck the sea-green tips of your hair into the loose knot at the nape of your neck, the motion automatic as
checking a tide table.
Your fingers close around the weatherproof notebook at your chest like a rosary.
The leather is still soft from years of being carried—days catalogued in pen smears and sticky residue, sketches of breakwaters and notes from
late-night council calls.
The brass compass on the chain against your throat is colder than you'd expected; you run your thumb along its edge and let the memory of your mother figure like a tide line
under everything.
The boardwalk is quieter than your memory wants it to be.
Once there were always more boats—a small flotilla bobbing, laughter spilling like nets.
Now there are fewer hulls, more patched planks and improvised floats.
A
neon sign over the bait shop sputters in hungry orange, painting the raindamps a color they don't remember being.
It buzzes and dies and buzzes again like an anxious heartbeat.
Somewhere ahead, Jonah leans against the rail.
thick black hair with sun-bleached tips, that steady, amber-brown look at the corners of his eyes when he smiles in a way that both welcomes and tests
you.
He’s in his oilskin coat patched at the elbows, hands shoved into pockets, a wool scarf tucked under his jaw against the salt.
He nods; it’s a small motion but the whole boardwalk tilts.
The
ache you’ve carried—the one that lives in the hollow where your father’s boat used to be—tightens like a line under strain.
You made it back before the meeting.
Figured you'd be more dramatic—grand entrance, fog parting, a gull dropping a policy brief on your head.
He lets out a short, wry laugh that doesn’t touch his eyes.
You wish.
Bus was late.
Fog moved faster than the timetable.
You force a smile; it feels like a muscle you haven't exercised in a while.
Feels smaller than you remember, doesn't it?
He jerks his chin toward the harbor.
'Or maybe that's just me getting older.'
smaller in boats, larger in the empty places.
You taste the memory of the storm as something tactile—shingles and salt and a voice on the radio
if you'd been here sooner, if you'd— The thought is a hooked line that snags on your throat and won't let go.
Smaller now.
But I'm here to help make sure it doesn't disappear altogether.
You feel the words before they leave your mouth, as if sculpting a promise in the mist.
Good.
Someone's got to remind Evelyn you can actually talk to people with plans that aren't just—
He waves a hand, searching, '—algorithms and whiteboards.'
You want to sharpen your answer.
He offers a familiar flint of his humor to strike up conversation and you almost take it, but the memory of leaving crowds the corners of the exchange with unresolved edges.
• Nod back at Jonah, keep it light
You tilt your head and give him a brief, deflecting nod.
'I'll bring the human part of the plan,' you tell him, letting the joke land where it needs to—on the surface.
Jonah relaxes a fraction; the old cadence returns, but the ache under it stays.
• Stare out at the water, quiet
You let your gaze drift to the harbor instead of answering.
The silence pulls Jonah's smile taut.
He doesn't push; he waits, and the waiting occupies a conversation of its own, full of things you can't say yet.
Continue main narrative
Jonah watches you choose, and whatever you do, he doesn't make it small.
sun-leathered skin, knotted fingers, a coat that looks older than most
of the harbor.
He hums something like a fragment of a tune and then lifts his head when he recognizes you, slow as the swell under the piers.
Well, would you look at that.
Back before the gulls stop talking.
He grunts a laugh.
It tastes like a memory of a market day.
'Don't go signing us away to the city planners yet.'
I don't plan to.
The words come out practical, sharpened by the years you spent learning how to make scale models and spreadsheets say what your heart wanted them to.
'I'm here to listen.
To measure.
To—' You stop; listening sounds smaller when you say it aloud, inadequate against maps and rising seas.
Bento eyes the compass at your throat, and for a second there's something almost kind—like recognition stripped bare of the lectures and the town's cautious humor.
Your ma's compass.
Hold it steady.
You'll need steady hands more than clever maps.
He taps the wood between you and him.
'And don't let 'em rush you.
Rushed things break.'
Evelyn passes by with mayoral placards folded under her arm—her scarf is a familiar weave of campaign colors now dulled by biennial meetings and tough funding cycles.
She greets Jonah with the short half-hug of two people who have shared too many storms and too many coffee pots.
Morning, Mira.
Good to see you back.
She smiles with the practiced warmth of someone who must carry more faces than she can really feed with the truth.
'Council's expecting some tough questions today.
There's talk of a city-level planner flying in—someone with a departmental badge.
Pamphlets were handed out at the dock this morning.'
She holds up a flier briefly; you see a logo—clean, severe type—and the implication of decisions made in rooms you don't know.
A city planner?
You let the question be open.
The name on the pamphlet is small and functional; the presence it implies is not.
Yes.
Pressures from the regional council.
Funding is tight.
They want a presentation—hard numbers, timelines.
Asha Verma's name came up; you might see her around the meeting.
The syllables of the name hang with the firmness of someone who makes decisions with a hand that doesn't tremble.
Asha Verma.
You let the name settle like a stone in the shallow pool of your thoughts.
The city planner with the departmental badge—her reputation precedes her, sharp as a surveyor's stake.
You do not know
fast decisions, the kind that make hard maps of people.
Jonah Reyes watches Evelyn's face for a beat and then looks back at you, an unreadable shadow crossing his features.
You and her will probably disagree about everything that isn't strictly measured.
Don't let it get in the way of the fishing rights talk, okay?
He says 'rights' like it's another tight knot he wants to keep from untangling.
I won't.
You mean it, but the word feels like a promise stretched over an uncertain map.
The pamphlet's logo—severe type against white—catches the neon's sputter.
You fingernail the cover of your notebook, feeling the dent where a pen used to live.
You think of scale bars and salt marsh cross-sections, of how
absolute, clean, and elegant in a way that excises the messy edges of people's lives.
• Press your thumb against the pendant and remember your father
your father's hands, rope-scented and sure, teaching you a knot.
The memory steadies you and tightens the ache into something you can carry.
• Open the notebook and run your eyes over tide charts
You pry the notebook open to a page of charts and markups.
Lines and arrows make sense in a way faces don't this morning.
It sharpens your focus—numbers are presentable, negotiable; emotion less so—until you have to translate it.
Continue main narrative
You choose, and the choice folds into you like a small stitch.
It doesn't erase anything; it gives shape to the ache.
about harvests that came
up thin, about the makeshift marsh-planting drive Kai ran last month, about Bento's stories of seas that used to be kinder.
The exchange loops and returns; Jonah teases, you counter with measured takes, Bento adds that
old-voice moral gravity, and Evelyn smooths for the sake of civic linens.
It's not a resolution.
It is a rehearsal for bureaucracy.
Promise me one thing.
He's suddenly quiet; the air rearranges around the syllable.
'If you go into that room wearing your mother’s compass like armor, don't let them convince you the only path is to leave everything we remember behind.'
I won't promise what I can't deliver.
The truth is thin; it's the plank under the tide—there, solid for now.
'But I'll argue for options that keep people here where it makes sense.
And if something can't be saved, we'll make sure moving is a choice, not a dictate.'
He looks at you long enough that the fog could have swallowed more of the harbor and you'd still feel his gaze.
You walk toward the Saltmarsh Research Lab—its greenhouse extension a sullen skin of glass, solar shades folded like patient eyelids.
The path takes you along the harbor edge; salt spray dots your jacket.
The sound of
the town rearranging its daily life—people sweeping boards, a generator's complaint, a child yelling—layers into a kind of communal rhythm that is both comfort and alarm.
You walk with the notebook in one hand and the compass resting against your sternum.
You rehearse what you'll say in the meeting—how living shorelines can absorb wave energy, how marsh restoration buys time, how community-led
projects keep livelihoods intact while we buy policy time.
The words feel both urgent and fragile.
You remember the late-night models you drew in grad school, the simulations that showed percentages and probabilities—sterile things until they
hit a place with faces and fishing nets and a history.
Dr.
Lian Zhou steps out of the lab doorway as you arrive, sleeves rolled and boots still salt-streaked.
She looks at you with the professional, almost maternal steadiness of someone who reads data and people with equal care.
You're Mira, right?
We were expecting you.
She nods at your notebook.
'Good—Evelyn called ahead.
We've pulled together some of the latest sediment cores and a preliminary elevation map.
Nothing final, but it'll frame today's conversation.'
Thank you.
I was hoping to compare the models against what people on the ground are seeing.
You hand the notebook closed, as if that tiny exchange could anchor science to memory.
That's exactly why you're here.
She offers a small smile, one that acknowledges the wear in your eyes.
'We need both—hard data and local knowledge.'
The lab's glass panes have salt trails poly-laced from storms past; inside, benches are cluttered with jars and monitors.
The greenhouse smells faintly of peat and wet compost, a softer breath than the boardwalk's diesel.
It is a place built to bridge—concrete models in a wooden hull.
a mayoral meeting that will decide whether Tidehaven's future is negotiated with community nuance or mapped in a ledger by someone who never
felt the weight of a net in their hands.
The fog presses at the windows like a question mark.
You tighten the strap of your notebook and feel the brass compass against your skin one last time.
You think about the possibility that engineering and memory might not simply coexist; you think about the compromises that will be demanded and the ones you are not ready to make.
The boardwalk recedes behind you
in a corridor of sound—Jonah's voice trailing, Bento's chuckle, Evelyn's measured steps.
Ahead, the lab's greenhouse glass is a palimpsest of light and salt.
The town is holding its breath.
You step forward into the lab and the meeting you prepared for waits like a tideline—inevitable, impatient.
Your thumb brushes the compass again.
The ache doesn't ease.
It becomes a decision you must carry through a room that prefers definitive answers.
You look up at the monitors where maps glow in teal and ochre.
A flier with a city-planner logo has already been pinned to the cork board near the door, a small, clinical emblem in a room smelling of peat and promise.
you as the woman who left and the woman who returned, both looking small and large all at once.
The day will require more than models and more than memory.
It will require the hard, honest work of turning both into a plan people can live inside.
You close your eyes for a heartbeat and let the fog from the boardwalk dissolve into the lab's steady light.
You step toward the place where data and promises will be laid out in front of a council that must choose.
You are ready to speak—but the meeting room door opens and the room waits for you to enter.

You stand at the threshold with the compass at your throat and the afternoon's meeting like a bruise under your ribs—choices, pressures, and the small human cost of every policy move orbiting in a cold, bureaucratic
gravity.
Someone calls your name.
The fog outside has not lifted; instead, it now feels like an audience holding its breath.
You sit with the compass warm against your sternum, its brass edge biting the fabric of your jacket when you shift.
The town hall smells of old wood, strong coffee, and the faint, sharp tang of
sea spray that has somehow found its way through the sash windows.
Your fingers worry the pendant under your collar the way you used to smooth the rigging lines on your father's boat — a small,
private ritual that steadies a body trying not to tremble.
Mira.
We're glad you could come.
The room's—' (she gestures at the papers) '—a mess.
And the council's tired, but they listen.
You reply with a nod that won't let itself be polite and say nothing more.
Words feel like promises you aren't sure you can keep.
Thank you, Mayor Sato.
I'll be brief.
The regional models indicate a twenty-five percent probability of an event above our current worst-case by 2046.
We need structural interventions now — flood gates at the harbor mouth, reinforced berms along the southern reaches, and a phased managed retreat for the lowest-lying parcels.
It is not ideal, but it is decisive.
precise, rapid, leaving nothing to linger.
It lands on you long enough to catalogue you as a node in her plan — engineer, asset, variable.
contour lines marching like soldiers.
You can see the logic in them.
You can see how, from
a distance, this plan could save many lives.
But the maps do not show who will lose afternoons on the boat, who will lose the smell of tar and fried fish, or the small rituals that
stitch Tidehaven together.
I can coordinate regional funding.
We implement now; we enforce zoning on the parcels at greatest risk.
We prioritize critical infrastructure.
The alternative is reactive spending and avoidable loss.
And what of stories?
Maps don't keep our nets from getting tangled, doctor.
They don't teach my grandson where the fish still come.
Managed retreat—' (he shakes his head) '—it's a long slow funeral for what we used to be.
Asha's expression tightens for the briefest second.
There is an unspoken ledger in her face, a memory perhaps, but she returns immediately to policy.
I am not asking for sacrifice for its own sake.
I'm asking for choices that prevent death.
We can design phased transitions.
It's about minimizing harm at scale.
system, scale, the clear lines of engineered certainty.
On the other: memory, practice, the scaffolding of a community's daily life.
• Finger the compass under your jacket
You close your hand around the brass and feel its familiar weight.
For a heartbeat the world narrows to that small solid thing between your fingers, and the temptation to answer with numbers and models withdraws into the shadow of the room.
• Glance at Jonah in the back row
Your eyes find Jonah like a magnet.
He sits with his hands knuckled over his knees, his oilskin coat creased and damp.
The smell of diesel and fish clings to him like a second skin; the sight of him ignites a low, guilty ache in your chest.

The hilltop models show both outcomes if either path runs alone.
If we invest in living shorelines everywhere without hard defenses, we see varied, localized improvements but exposure during extreme surge events increases.
If we go hard on infrastructure, we reduce acute risk but lose social fabric and displacement spikes.
Hybrid approaches could be viable but require meticulous phasing and social programs.
Her voice isn't a judgement; it's a ledger.
The numbers line up like paces toward an inevitable shore.
You tell 'em, Dr.
Lian,' (he says, half-smile, half-ace-in-the-hole) 'But the sea's not a model you can box.
We patch the shoreline with living roots and rock where we can.
We teach a kid to read a tide and she's worth more than any gated berm.
He stands by the aisle, voice lifted, as if the simple act of speaking could pull the tide back a little.
Mr.
Reyes, living shorelines are excellent where they can be maintained and where the landscape allows.
Tidehaven's southern parcels are below threshold for such measures to be reliably effective.
They will require maintenance budgets and expertise you may not be able to sustain.
We can sustain them.
You don't know us.
We have folks here willing to learn and to sweat for it.
We don't want to be told to retreat because you think it's cleaner to draw a line on a map.
Please.
We need constructive proposals, not indictments.
Asha, can you outline what 'phased' would mean in terms of community involvement?
Jonah, can you give us a realistic maintenance schedule for a pilot marsh restoration?
the engineer who left and returned, asked to build the bridge between policy and people.
Mira, girl.
You know the difference between a tide that takes and a tide that teaches.
What part of us do you think is worth saving?
You consider the question.

It is less about coastlines than about the ledger in your chest — the debt you pay to the memory of a father who once taught you to knot, to stand in wind and watch a horizon that took more than it gave.
I came back because I couldn't stand sitting in a lecture hall while my town changed.
I thought I could translate models into plans that people could live with.
I still think that.
But I can't—' (you stop; the admission is heavy) '—I can't promise a perfect solution.
No one promised perfection.
I'm offering a pathway that averts the greatest harm.
If we wait to achieve consensus on culturally perfect solutions, we will lose chances to prevent large-scale loss.
And if you bulldoze everything you call 'at risk' because it's easier to manage, you'll bulldoze people's lives with it.
People are not variables, Asha.
We're stubborn, yes, but we are also the ones who will keep any solution alive.
• Offer to design a hybrid pilot today
You stand up before you think better of the politics.

'I'll draft a phased pilot that pairs engineered berms in critical nodes with living shoreline buffers and community maintenance.' The room stills — hope, suspicion, and the machinery of bureaucracy jostling for position.
• Stay silent and let them decide
You clamp your mouth shut.
Silence swells in your chest like a tide.
The room fills itself with plans and accusations without your guidance; it is swift and cold in your absence.
Continue the scene
Mira, if you steer a pilot, will you engage the community?
Will you help write the social supports?
We need someone credible to hold the science to the people.
You imagine the long hours, the meetings that bleed into late nights, the paperwork, the compromises.
You imagine Jonah's hands hauling willow stakes at dawn, Bento's stories threaded into planting plans, Dr.
Lian's models humming in
you are one person between a town and a decision that will be measured in decades.
Your throat tightens.
The compass under your jacket is a small warm doubt.
You think of your father's boat — of how a line frayed and how you, young and clumsy, failed to see the knot that might have saved it.
The guilt is a weathered thing; it has hardened into a carefulness you are not sure you can abandon.
If you choose to shape community projects, understand there will be trade-offs.
Lives may be protected at the cost of certain ways of living.
That honesty needs to be part of your engagement.
And if you choose retreat, know you'll be writing an obituary for things people still love.
Either way, someone will say you didn't try hard enough.
We can model stress scenarios.
We can optimize allocations.
But data can't hold hands at the shoreline.
The council chamber grows heavier; voices fragment into smaller, urgent threads.
You are asked, implicitly and explicitly, to be the hinge.
Council needs a recommendation by next week.
A pilot, a maintenance plan, or a commitment to a phased infrastructure proposal.
Mira—will you shepherd this?
You step out onto the boardwalk after the meeting.
The air is colder than the town hall's amber light, a salt-breathed bite that wakes your skin.
The sea spreads out in a thin silver threat, and
fog fingers the edges of the harbor.
The boardwalk planks creak under your boots like an old voice.
You stand alone with the compass at your throat.
The harbor reflects a line of sky that looks suspiciously like a question.
You think of models and of mangroves, of gates and of gardens; you think of the people who would be asked to move and those who would be asked to hold fast.
The decision to steer
the pilot or to defer to engineered certainty is no longer theoretical.
It is a ledger you will sign with your hours and your name.
your hands still hold the memory of the meeting's arguments, of Jonah's stubbornness, Asha's clinical clarity, Evelyn's weary faith.
The fog makes the harbor anonymous and
dangerous at once.
You can feel a choice forming like a bruise under skin — tender, inevitable.
It is not resolved here.
It waits, patient and heavy, for the shape you will finally give it.
You stand with the field jacket pulled tight around your shoulders, the damp pressing through the collar like a question.
The bell in the town hall still vibrates in your chest; the meeting is a bruise
beneath your ribs.
The harbor is a sheet of pewter, restless and patient.
Each creak beneath your boots sounds like a ledger closing and opening at once.
The air tastes of old rope and iron; gulls call somewhere beyond the fog but their shapes are only noise.
The boardwalk rail is cold where your hand rests.
In your palm the town's lines feel
where people fish, where Bento's knees know the shallows, where the younger families
park their boats.
Every seam is someone’s history, and every history is a possible loss.
You exhale.
The compass at your throat—a warm, familiar weight—suddenly feels like a metronome for decisions you didn't ask to make.

Bento's voice replays in the hallway like gravel You left, Mira.' It wasn't accusatory so much as a fact that settled like silt.
You don't tidy it away; you let it lie heavy.
It sits alongside Asha's steadiness, Jonah’s low-voiced stubbornness, Dr.
Lian's charts, and Evelyn's tired request—'Mira—will you shepherd this?
• Call Jonah now — hear his pitch again
Your thumb hesitates on his number.
You imagine his voice, the rasp of the harbor in it, the impatience turned warm if he thinks you're with him.
Part of you aches toward that straightforward, neighborly certainty.

Lian's models again

Numbers are honest in their own way; they don't offer forgiveness, only projections.
The laptop hums in your mind like a small engine of truth.

the harbor's flat breath,
the distant clank of a mooring line, the creak of an old sign.

You walk back toward the town hall because leadership is a place that expects you at its thresholds.
The corridor smells faintly of coffee and stale paper—petitions pinned like moth wings to the corkboards.
Bento is
there, stooped against the wall, hands threaded in each other as if holding the past together.
His face has the map of the weather in it.
You left, Mira.
I know.
(He studies you.) 'We needed you, girl.
The sea doesn't wait for apologies.'
I didn't think—
We still do.
But it changes your standing when you go and come back.
People remember what you chose when the waters rise.
You swallow and press your palm to the corridor's cool tile.
Bento's hands are like barnacled anchors.
He smells of smoked fish and oil smoke; the scent tethers you to a childhood of boat decks and
his stories.
You want to tell him how much you carry—how you'd stayed up nights redrawing marsh lines, how you've been trying to reconcile engineering with memory—but the words want to be less clinical and more
human, and you don't know which he'll take as a plan and which as confession.
• Tell Bento you left to learn how to help
His eyes water for a second—friction turning to something like understanding—and then he huffs softly.
'Learning won't stop the sea.' The pause that follows is both warning and blessing.
• Admit you still feel guilty and ask for his trust
Bento's jaw tightens.
He studies your hands, then nods once, slow.
'Trust is earned, Mira.
Start earning it.' It sounds like a task; tasks are what he gives you now.
Continue to 'You leave Bento with a promise you aren't yet sure you can keep.'
You leave Bento with a promise you aren't yet sure you can keep.
His words settle into the lining of your jacket like grit.
The lab feels colder than the corridor.
Here, under LEDs and the clean geometry of maps, the fight turns into numbers.
Dr.
Lian Zhou stands over a workstation, her palms flat on the table like she’s
five-year scenarios tracked in jagged teal—wetland area shrinking into sharper teeth with each tick.
The models show the marsh stepping back faster than our last run suggested.
Salt intrusion accelerates loss if we don't change hydrology.' (She taps a jagged line.) 'That's the baseline without intervention.
You lean closer, the light catching the tired curve beneath your eyes.
The graphs look inevitable and small at once—numbers that already belonged to futures you refused to name when you were younger.
How confident are these ranges?
We're within a tight confidence interval for sea-level acceleration and sediment deficits.
Intervention windows close—some in as little as three years for certain shoals.
We can buy time with soft measures, but only if they're scaled and maintained.
Asha Verma enters the lab with her coat clipped at the shoulders, an argument already forming in the angular set of her mouth.
Mira, we can't rely on volunteer crews and pole plantings to hold a coastline when the sea's behaving like a bulldozer.
We need enforceable zones, a sea-gate pilot, reinforced berms.
I need someone to lead the technical deployment.
You look at Asha's eyes—steel-blue and exact.
There's no theatrical cruelty there, only a steady, bureaucratic cruelty of necessity that learned its rules through loss.
Enforceable zones mean people uprooted.' (The sentence tastes like salt.) 'Sea-gates mean capital, construction, and political friction.
It's not purely technical.
(Quiet but firm.) 'No solution is purely technical, Mira.
But neither is this a sentimental choice.
If we act slowly, we risk rapid failure.
I want someone who can stand in the centre of this and make the hard calls.'
Jonah Reyes appears in the doorway before you can answer, the lab's fluorescent light flattening the warmth in his eyes but not taking it away.
Hard calls, yeah.
But the town's hands should be on those projects.
We know the tides—Bento taught me where poles hold and where they don't.
We can rebuild poles and marshes.
People will come.
We don't need a machine deciding which houses are worth saving.
his scent of wool and wet rope, his steady, stubborn warmth against Asha's surgical clarity.
The temperature of the conversation drops and bakes in the same moment.
People die when systems fail, Jonah.
Emotion won't stop the waterline.
And policy without people is a graveyard of machines.
Who pays for the upkeep when the funding dries?
Who takes responsibility when a berm fails?
You speak of efficiency; we speak of belonging.
(You feel the old ache—guilt braided with determination.) 'Both of you are right and wrong in different ways.
Asha, your timeframes are tight—you're trying to prevent collapse.
Jonah, your pride in the town's methods
is what keeps the place whole.
Dr.
Lian, your models close windows.
Bento's memory keeps us honest.
Evelyn wants a recommendation.'
(Entrances with the tired dignity of someone who must distribute hope with tight hands.) 'Mira.
We're listening.
Council wants a recommendation by next week.
Pilot, maintenance plan, or phased infrastructure.
The town waits.
You know everyone in this room.
What do you recommend?'
The question lands like a tide on rock—patient, inevitable.
Outside, the harbor breathes.
Inside, the machines hum.
There's a way to structure pilots to produce defensible data.
We can test a living shoreline at one shoal and a managed berm at another.
But we need clarity on what success metrics will be used and who will commit to maintenance.
You mean trials.
Trials take time.
People in the interim will keep losing ground.
Trials without firm fallback plans create false hope.

(You close your eyes because the noise is too loud inside your skull: Bento's small condemnation, your father's ghost on an empty deck, Jonah's camera shutter capturing a harbor that keeps getting smaller, Asha's hard-lipped urgency.
The models on your laptop glow behind your eyelids like watchful fish.)
(Narration)
You feel the town as a fault line beneath your boots—one brittle seam where values pull opposite ways.
You don't just hold scientific knowledge here; you carry the moral ledger of who pays what cost.
Your
calluses from crawling on decks, the faint scar from a rope that slipped years ago—you remember your father when the boat was taken; you remember leaving.
All of that counts
now in a way that charts never capture.

a relationship, a tradition, a sense of security.'
a relationship, a tradition, a sense of security.
Nothing is purely technical.
Nothing is purely sentimental.
Asha steps closer, lowering her voice so the wires and monitors cannot overhear her.
Mira, I need someone who's decisive.
This town needs a leader who can navigate politics and spreadsheets.
Will you lead the deployment?
(Jonah's hand brushes the back of a chair as if readying himself to stand and plant stakes in the mud, the image of his wrists callused around rope knotting in your chest.)
If you choose people, you'll be choosing to fight with them.
You'll get your hands dirty.
You'll also be choosing to take more time—and possibly more risk.
But you'll be doing it with people who remember why each stone matters.
Council needs a single recommendation.
The public hearing will be brutal.
Whatever you choose, own it or we'll flounder.
Tidehaven can't afford a leader who is indecisive.
You inhale and the lab's sterile lights feel like a spotlight.
The wind beyond the panes feels like an accusation.
Your chest tightens as if sea air itself is compressing the space around your heart.
(Narration)
You replay every possible outcome like a tide chart—what you salvage, what you concede.
You picture Bento walking the reclaimed marsh and nodding or shaking his head.
You picture Jonah's hands wrapped around a pole he
helped plant; you picture Asha's plans being drawn up and signed and becoming law.
You imagine telling families that their gardens are doomed or that their houses will be moved.
The ledger fills with names.

We'll plant what we can.
Night settles with a soft hush.
The town is patchworked but held.
The model will be taught, copied, improved.
Scholarships will be named; planners will come to lecture here.
Tidehaven will be a case study in
the region is safer, protocols are tighter, the charts are clearer.
But you have also counted private losses tonight—a mooring rope knotted into memory, a porch rug ruined, a retired cutter with paint flaked like exit wounds.
The institutional gain sits beside the private pain like two
weights balanced on an imperfect scale.
You think of your father's boat, of the way it used to creak in the harbor at dawn, and you let the grief that you carried alone in meetings finally
come up like tidewater.
You pull the compass from beneath your collar and hold it out to the night.
You could wish for absolution, for a single fix that erases the houses that flooded and the boats that were retired.
You know there is no such fix.
There is only the long work of repair, of listening, of accepting that some losses will remain as scars, as place-markers for what was and what cannot be reclaimed.
You close your hand.
The compass clicks shut.
The town breathes around you—hurt and hopeful, marching on.
You climb the path alone this time, boots silent on damp gravel.
The wind has taken the edges off the day and left the core—what was necessary and what was costly—raw in your chest.
From the cliffs you can see the whole—terrible, beautiful, stubborn.
You speak aloud, though no one answers except the sea.
We did something that saved more than it cost.
We also failed people we wanted to protect.
Both are true.
Both are ours.
You let the sentence settle.
It does not fix anything.
It does not make reparation.
It names.
A small light in the harbor—someone's lantern—blinks like a promise.
You tuck the compass back beneath your jacket and feel, unexpectedly, a quiet steadiness.
The town will be remade in ways both chosen and unchosen.
You will keep doing your part, imperfectly, relentlessly.
You turn from the cliffs and head back down toward the town.
There will be meetings tomorrow, reports, calls from neighboring mayors asking for your playbook.
There will also be a woman who wants to sit
in the cold with you and remember her husband.
There will be a boy who needs a new treehouse, a retired cutter that needs paint, and a neighborhood that must be helped to dry out its
memories.
You breathe in the salt air, and for the first time in a long time the breath feels like an agreement between the past and the possible.
The ending is not a triumph or a defeat;
it is a ledger made of many small entries—some credits, some debts—that you will carry forward.
THE END
